request.question: The 2009 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXV ING Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on which date, Sebastian Vettel came third after starting from eighth on the grid?

title: 2000 Belgian Grand Prix
2014 Japanese Grand Prix
2016 Belgian Grand Prix
2015 Belgian Grand Prix
2014 Belgian Grand Prix
2008 Belgian Grand Prix
2005 Belgian Grand Prix
2011 Belgian Grand Prix
2009 Belgian Grand Prix
Sebastian Vettel
sentences: The 2000 Belgian Grand Prix (formally, the LVIII Foster's Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 27 August 2000 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps, Stavelot, Belgium.
It was the thirteenth race of the 2000 Formula One season, and the 58th Belgian Grand Prix.
The 44-lap race was won by McLaren driver Mika Häkkinen, who started from the pole position.
Michael Schumacher finished second in a Ferrari, and Williams driver Ralf Schumacher was third.
The 2014 Japanese Grand Prix (formally the 2014 Formula 1 Japanese Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 5 October at the Suzuka Circuit in Suzuka, Mie.
It was the fifteenth round of the 2014 Formula One season and the 30th Japanese Grand Prix held as part of the Formula One World Championship.
The 44-lap race was won by Mercedes driver Lewis Hamilton, who started from second position.
His teammate, Nico Rosberg, finished second and Red Bull Racing driver Sebastian Vettel came in third.
It was Hamilton's eighth victory of the season, his first at Suzuka and the 30th of his Formula One career.
The 2016 Belgian Grand Prix (formally known as the 2016 Formula 1 Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 28 August 2016 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.
It was the thirteenth round of the 2016 Formula One season, and marks the seventy-second running of the Belgian Grand Prix and the fifty-ninth time the race was held at Spa-Francorchamps.
The 2015 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the 2015 Formula 1 Shell Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 23 August 2015 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.
It was the eleventh round of the 2015 Formula One season, and the 71st Belgian Grand Prix.
The 2014 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the 2014 Formula 1 Shell Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 24 August 2014 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.
It was the twelfth round of the 2014 Formula One season, and the 70th Belgian Grand Prix.
The 44-lap race was won by Daniel Ricciardo for the Red Bull Racing team, after starting from fifth position.
Nico Rosberg finished second in a Mercedes, with Valtteri Bottas third for the Williams team.
The 2008 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXIV ING Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 7 September 2008 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps near the town of Spa, Belgium.
It was the 13th race of the 2008 Formula One season.

The 44-lap race was won by Felipe Massa for the Ferrari team, after the initial winner, McLaren driver Lewis Hamilton, was penalised for cutting a chicane and gaining an advantage over Ferrari's Kimi Räikkönen.
The 2005 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXII Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 11 September 2005 at Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.
It was the sixteenth round of the 2005 Formula One season and the sixty-second Belgian Grand Prix.
The 44-lap race was won by Kimi Räikkönen for the McLaren team, from a second position start.
Fernando Alonso finished second in a Renault, with Jenson Button third for the BAR team.
The 2011 Belgian Grand Prix, formally the 2011 Formula 1 Shell Belgian Grand Prix, was a Formula One motor race that was held on 28 August 2011, at Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.
It was the twelfth round of the 2011 Formula One season and the 67th Belgian Grand Prix to be held.
The 44-lap race was won by Red Bull's Sebastian Vettel, the drivers' championship leader, after starting from pole position.
Vettel's teammate Mark Webber finished in second place, and Jenson Button completed the podium in third position for McLaren.
The 2009 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXV ING Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 30 August 2009 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps near the town of Spa, Belgium.
It was the 12th race of the 2009 Formula One season.
The race was contested over 44 laps, with an overall race distance of 308.2 km (191.5 mi).
The race was won by 2007 world champion Kimi Räikkönen of Ferrari after starting from sixth on the grid.
Giancarlo Fisichella of Force India came second after starting from the team's first pole position.
Sebastian Vettel came third after starting from eighth on the grid.
Sebastian Vettel (] ; born 3 July 1987) is a German racing driver currently driving in Formula One for Scuderia Ferrari.

He is a four-time Formula One World Champion, having won the championship in , , and with Red Bull Racing, therefore being among the most successful F1 drivers of all time, as he is one of only four drivers to have won four or more drivers' titles.
He is regarded by fellow and former drivers as one of the greatest Formula One drivers in the history of the sport.
Vettel signed a three-year contract with Ferrari upon his arrival, a contract later extended until the end of 2020.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: The 2009 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXV ING Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on which date, Sebastian Vettel came third after starting from eighth on the grid?
hop1_docs: 2009 Belgian Grand Prix | The 2009 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXV ING Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 30 August 2009 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps near the town of Spa, Belgium.  It was the 12th race of the 2009 Formula One season.  The race was contested over 44 laps, with an overall race distance of 308.2 km (191.5 mi).  The race was won by 2007 world champion Kimi Räikkönen of Ferrari after starting from sixth on the grid.  Giancarlo Fisichella of Force India came second after starting from the team's first pole position.  Sebastian Vettel came third after starting from eighth on the grid.

2011 Belgian Grand Prix | The 2011 Belgian Grand Prix, formally the 2011 Formula 1 Shell Belgian Grand Prix, was a Formula One motor race that was held on 28 August 2011, at Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.  It was the twelfth round of the 2011 Formula One season and the 67th Belgian Grand Prix to be held.  The 44-lap race was won by Red Bull's Sebastian Vettel, the drivers' championship leader, after starting from pole position.  Vettel's teammate Mark Webber finished in second place, and Jenson Button completed the podium in third position for McLaren.

2011 Japanese Grand Prix | The 2011 Japanese Grand Prix (formally the XXXVII Japanese Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race that was held on 9 October 2011 at the Suzuka Circuit in Suzuka, Japan.  It was the fifteenth round of the 2011 Formula One season and the thirty-eighth time the Japanese Grand Prix had been held.  The 53-lap race was won by McLaren's Jenson Button, after he started from second on the grid.  Fernando Alonso finished in second place for Scuderia Ferrari, and Sebastian Vettel completed the podium, with third, for Red Bull Racing.

2011 Hungarian Grand Prix | The 2011 Hungarian Grand Prix, formally the XXVII Eni Magyar Nagydíj, was a Formula One motor race that was held on 31 July 2011 at the Hungaroring in Mogyoród, near Budapest, Hungary It was the eleventh round of the 2011 Formula One season, and the 27th Hungarian Grand Prix.  The 70-lap race was won by McLaren's Jenson Button, in his 200th Grand Prix start, after starting from third on the grid.  Championship leader Sebastian Vettel, who started the race from pole position, finished in second place for Red Bull, and Fernando Alonso completed the podium in third position for Ferrari.

2011 Chinese Grand Prix | The 2011 Chinese Grand Prix (formally the 2011 Formula 1 UBS Chinese Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 17 April 2011 at the Shanghai International Circuit in Shanghai, People's Republic of China.  It was the third round of the 2011 Formula One season.  The 56-lap race was won by McLaren driver Lewis Hamilton after starting from third on the grid, also becoming the first ever multiple winner of the Chinese Grand Prix.  Sebastian Vettel finished second in a Red Bull, having started from pole position, and teammate Mark Webber completed the podium, in third place having started eighteenth.

2011 European Grand Prix | The 2011 European Grand Prix (formally the LV Grand Prix of Europe) was a Formula One motor race held on 26 June 2011 at the Valencia Street Circuit in Valencia, Spain.  The race, which was the eighth round of the 2011 Formula One season, was won by defending world drivers' champion and championship leader Sebastian Vettel, who was driving a Red Bull car.  Vettel, who started from pole position, also recorded the fastest lap of the race on lap 53, giving him a hat-trick.  After a race-long battle, Ferrari driver Fernando Alonso and Vettel's teammate Mark Webber finished the race in second and third respectively.  The race was notable for having the fewest retirements and the most finishers ever in a Formula One Grand Prix.

2014 Japanese Grand Prix | The 2014 Japanese Grand Prix (formally the 2014 Formula 1 Japanese Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 5 October at the Suzuka Circuit in Suzuka, Mie.  It was the fifteenth round of the 2014 Formula One season and the 30th Japanese Grand Prix held as part of the Formula One World Championship.  The 44-lap race was won by Mercedes driver Lewis Hamilton, who started from second position.  His teammate, Nico Rosberg, finished second and Red Bull Racing driver Sebastian Vettel came in third.  It was Hamilton's eighth victory of the season, his first at Suzuka and the 30th of his Formula One career.

hop2_docs: 2010 Belgian Grand Prix | The 2010 Belgian Grand Prix was the 66th Belgian Grand Prix and the thirteenth round of the 2010 Formula One season.  It was held at Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium on 29 August 2010.  Williams driver Rubens Barrichello celebrated his 300th Grand Prix.  Lewis Hamilton claimed victory, to take the championship lead from pole-sitter Mark Webber, who finished second.  Robert Kubica finished in third position, taking his last Formula One podium finish to date.

2009 Belgian Grand Prix | The 2009 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXV ING Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 30 August 2009 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps near the town of Spa, Belgium.  It was the 12th race of the 2009 Formula One season.  The race was contested over 44 laps, with an overall race distance of 308.2 km (191.5 mi).  The race was won by 2007 world champion Kimi Räikkönen of Ferrari after starting from sixth on the grid.  Giancarlo Fisichella of Force India came second after starting from the team's first pole position.  Sebastian Vettel came third after starting from eighth on the grid.

1925 Belgian Grand Prix | The 1925 Belgian Grand Prix was a Grand Prix motor race held at Spa-Francorchamps on 28 June 1925.  This was the first ever grand prix held at Spa, and the first ever Belgian Grand Prix.

1934 Belgian Grand Prix | The 1934 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the V Grand Prix de Belgique) was a Grand Prix motor race, which was run on 29 July 1934 in Spa-Francorchamps, Belgium.  The race lasted 596.05 km (14.90 km x 40 laps).  It was the fifth running of the Belgian Grand Prix.

2013 Belgian Grand Prix | The 2013 Belgian Grand Prix (formally known as the 2013 Formula 1 Shell Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race on 25 August 2013 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.  It was the eleventh round of the 2013 season, and the 69th running of the Belgian Grand Prix.

2015 Belgian Grand Prix | The 2015 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the 2015 Formula 1 Shell Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 23 August 2015 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.  It was the eleventh round of the 2015 Formula One season, and the 71st Belgian Grand Prix.

British Grand Prix | The British Grand Prix is a race in the calendar of the FIA Formula One World Championship.  It is currently held at the Silverstone Circuit near the village of Silverstone in Northamptonshire in England.  The British and Italian Grands Prix are the only Formula One World Championship Grands Prix staged continuously since the championship was introduced in 1950 (the Monaco and Belgian Grands Prix have missed a few seasons since hosting races in the 1950 inaugural season).  It was designated the European Grand Prix five times between 1950 and 1977, when this title was an honorary designation given each year to one Grand Prix race in Europe.  All British Grands Prix dating back to 1926 have been held in England; where the British motor racing industry is primarily located.
response.summary_2: The 2009 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXV ING Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 30 August 2009. Sebastian Vettel came third after starting from eighth on the grid.
